content.text: The Purpose of a Structured Review Cadence

A budget cap enforced in real time tells you when you have run out of money. A forecast-versus-actual review cadence tells you whether you are going to run out before month end — and why. The two mechanisms are complementary: guardrails are the circuit breaker; reviews are the steering wheel. Without a structured cadence, teams discover budget problems only when the guardrail fires, at which point the options for response are limited.

The cadence described here operates on three horizons: weekly (operational), monthly (tactical), and quarterly (strategic). Each horizon has a different audience, a different level of analytical depth, and a different set of decisions it is expected to produce.

The simplest forecast that is useful in practice: divide spend to date by elapsed days to get a daily burn rate, then multiply by the number of days remaining in the month. This linear extrapolation is reliable when usage is stable, but it understates end-of-month spend for teams that run heavy batch jobs on weekends or quarter-end reporting cycles. A simple adjustment computes a day-of-week weighted rate from the trailing four weeks.

from __future__ import annotations
import datetime
from collections import defaultdict
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class DailySpend:
    date:     datetime.date
    team:     str
    cost_usd: float

@dataclass
class ForecastResult:
    team:              str
    period_month:      str     # "2025-06"
    spent_to_date:     float
    cap_usd:           float
    days_elapsed:      int
    days_remaining:    int
    linear_forecast:   float   # simple linear EOM estimate
    weighted_forecast: float   # day-of-week weighted EOM estimate
    forecast_variance_pct: float  # (weighted - cap) / cap

    @property
    def is_at_risk(self) -> bool:
        return self.weighted_forecast > self.cap_usd * 0.90

def forecast_eom_spend(
    history: list[DailySpend],
    team: str,
    cap_usd: float,
    today: Optional[datetime.date] = None,
) -> ForecastResult:
    today        = today or datetime.date.today()
    period_start = today.replace(day=1)
    # Last day of month
    next_month   = (today.replace(day=28) + datetime.timedelta(days=4)).replace(day=1)
    period_end   = next_month - datetime.timedelta(days=1)

    team_history = [r for r in history if r.team == team]
    spent = sum(r.cost_usd for r in team_history
                if period_start <= r.date <= today)
    days_elapsed   = (today - period_start).days + 1
    days_remaining = (period_end - today).days

    # Linear forecast
    daily_rate     = spent / days_elapsed if days_elapsed > 0 else 0.0
    linear_eom     = spent + daily_rate * days_remaining

    # Day-of-week weighted rate: compute avg spend per weekday from last 4 weeks
    dow_totals: dict[int, list[float]] = defaultdict(list)
    four_weeks_ago = today - datetime.timedelta(weeks=4)
    for r in team_history:
        if four_weeks_ago <= r.date < period_start:
            dow_totals[r.date.weekday()].append(r.cost_usd)
    dow_avg = {
        dow: sum(vals) / len(vals)
        for dow, vals in dow_totals.items() if vals
    }
    remaining_days_spend = 0.0
    for i in range(1, days_remaining + 1):
        d   = today + datetime.timedelta(days=i)
        dow = d.weekday()
        remaining_days_spend += dow_avg.get(dow, daily_rate)
    weighted_eom = spent + remaining_days_spend

    return ForecastResult(
        team              = team,
        period_month      = today.strftime("%Y-%m"),
        spent_to_date     = spent,
        cap_usd           = cap_usd,
        days_elapsed      = days_elapsed,
        days_remaining    = days_remaining,
        linear_forecast   = linear_eom,
        weighted_forecast = weighted_eom,
        forecast_variance_pct = (weighted_eom - cap_usd) / cap_usd,
    )

The weekly review is a 30-minute FinOps team standup. Its purpose is early-warning: catching teams whose spend is diverging from forecast before it becomes a crisis. The agenda is entirely data-driven — the meeting opens with the weekly report and spends its time on the flagged outliers, not a round-robin of every team.
Confirm report was delivered to all teams
Root cause and reforecast if needed

from dataclasses import dataclass

VARIANCE_WARN_PCT = 0.10   # flag teams whose forecast is >10% above cap

@dataclass
class WeeklyReportRow:
    team:              str
    spent_to_date:     float
    cap_usd:           float
    weighted_forecast: float
    forecast_vs_cap:   float    # positive = over forecast
    flagged:           bool

def build_weekly_report(
    forecasts: list[ForecastResult],
) -> list[WeeklyReportRow]:
    rows = []
    for f in forecasts:
        variance = (f.weighted_forecast - f.cap_usd) / f.cap_usd
        rows.append(WeeklyReportRow(
            team              = f.team,
            spent_to_date     = f.spent_to_date,
            cap_usd           = f.cap_usd,
            weighted_forecast = f.weighted_forecast,
            forecast_vs_cap   = variance,
            flagged           = abs(variance) > VARIANCE_WARN_PCT or f.is_at_risk,
        ))
    return sorted(rows, key=lambda r: r.forecast_vs_cap, reverse=True)

The month-end review is a 60-minute session with FinOps, finance, and team leads. It has two outputs: a signed-off variance analysis explaining why actuals differed from the prior-month forecast, and a revised forecast for the coming month. Variance analysis should always chase root causes, not just acknowledge the numbers.
Dev team chose larger model by default
Batch job ran earlier than planned

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class VarianceCategory(Enum):
    VOLUME     = "volume"
    PRICE      = "price"
    MIX        = "mix"
    EFFICIENCY = "efficiency"
    TIMING     = "timing"

@dataclass
class MonthEndVariance:
    team:             str
    period_month:     str
    forecast_usd:     float
    actual_usd:       float
    variance_usd:     float   # actual - forecast
    variance_pct:     float
    category:         VarianceCategory
    root_cause:       str
    action:           str

def decompose_variance(
    prior_forecast: ForecastResult,
    actual_spend:   float,
    avg_tokens_per_call_actual:   int,
    avg_tokens_per_call_forecast: int,
    actual_call_count:   int,
    forecast_call_count: int,
) -> VarianceCategory:
    """Heuristic: pick the dominant variance category."""
    volume_delta    = (actual_call_count - forecast_call_count) / max(forecast_call_count, 1)
    efficiency_delta = (avg_tokens_per_call_actual - avg_tokens_per_call_forecast) / max(avg_tokens_per_call_forecast, 1)
    if abs(volume_delta) > abs(efficiency_delta):
        return VarianceCategory.VOLUME
    return VarianceCategory.EFFICIENCY

The quarterly review (QBR) extends the scope from cost control to business value. It answers: are we getting the expected return on GenAI spend? Which features are expensive relative to their revenue or user value contribution? The audience includes the CFO or finance VP in addition to FinOps and team leads.
Right-size model selection by use case

Review meetings are only as good as the artefacts that feed them. A weekly cron job runs the forecast queries, generates the variance report, and sends it to team distribution lists. The report lands in inboxes before the Monday review, so the meeting can skip data-gathering and focus on decisions.

import datetime
        import smtplib
        from email.mime.text import MIMEText

        def generate_weekly_report_text(
            rows: list[WeeklyReportRow],
            period_month: str,
        ) -> str:
            today = datetime.date.today()
            lines = [
                f"GenAI FinOps Weekly Report — {today.isoformat()}",
                f"Period: {period_month}",
                "",
                f"{'Team':<20} {'Spent':>10} {'Cap':>10} {'EOM Forecast':>14} {'vs Cap':>8} {'Status':>10}",
                "-" * 74,
            ]
            for r in rows:
                status = "FLAGGED" if r.flagged else "ok"
                lines.append(
                    f"{r.team:<20} "
                    f"${r.spent_to_date:>9,.2f} "
                    f"${r.cap_usd:>9,.2f} "
                    f"${r.weighted_forecast:>13,.2f} "
                    f"{r.forecast_vs_cap:>+7.1%} "
                    f"{status:>10}"
                )
            flagged = [r for r in rows if r.flagged]
            if flagged:
                lines += ["", "FLAGGED TEAMS — investigate before Monday review:"]
                for r in flagged:
                    direction = "OVER" if r.forecast_vs_cap > 0 else "UNDER"
                    lines.append(
                        f"  {r.team}: forecast {direction} cap by {abs(r.forecast_vs_cap):.1%} "
                        f"(EOM est. ${r.weighted_forecast:,.2f} vs cap ${r.cap_usd:,.2f})"
                    )
            lines += ["", "Full dashboard: https://finops.internal/genai/weekly"]
            return "
".join(lines)

        def send_weekly_report(
            rows: list[WeeklyReportRow],
            period_month: str,
            recipients: list[str],
            smtp_host: str,
            from_addr: str,
        ) -> None:
            body = generate_weekly_report_text(rows, period_month)
            msg  = MIMEText(body, "plain")
            msg["Subject"] = (
                f"[GenAI FinOps] Weekly report — {datetime.date.today().isoformat()} "
                f"({sum(1 for r in rows if r.flagged)} flagged)"
            )
            msg["From"] = from_addr
            msg["To"]   = ", ".join(recipients)
            with smtplib.SMTP(smtp_host, 587) as smtp:
                smtp.starttls()
                smtp.sendmail(from_addr, recipients, msg.as_string())

A forecasting process that is never measured never improves. Track forecast accuracy as a FinOps programme KPI: the percentage by which the end-of-month forecast (made at week 2 of the month) differed from the actual close. The target is ±15%. Persistent error above ±20% signals a flawed forecasting model or an uncontrolled driver that is not being captured in the forecast inputs.

A structured forecast-versus-actual review cadence is the management layer that sits above real-time guardrails. Weekly reviews catch divergences early; month-end reviews produce root-cause analysis and revised forecasts; quarterly reviews align GenAI spend with business value and re-baseline annual budgets.

content: Use day-of-week weighted burn rates rather than simple linear extrapolation — batch-heavy teams skew heavily toward certain weekdays and linear extrapolation systematically under-forecasts their month-end spend.
Track forecast accuracy as a programme KPI (target: week-2 forecast within ±15% of actuals) — persistent errors signal a broken forecasting model, not just normal variance.
Decompose variance into categories (volume, price, mix, efficiency) so teams can take targeted action rather than just knowing they spent more than planned.
Automate the weekly report generation and delivery — a review meeting that spends time pulling data is a review meeting that runs out of time for decisions.
The quarterly review is the forum for strategic decisions: killing expensive low-value features, right-sizing model selection, and renegotiating provider contracts.
